game_genre_context: Idle / clicker game (Adventure Capitalist, Egg Inc.) where daily login isn't enough — players need 'check-in throughout day' loops. Multi-touch-point dailies (morning, lunch, evening) drive 3-4 sessions per day vs single-touchpoint games' 1.
title: Multi-Touchpoint Daily Quest System with Time-Window Sub-Tasks
design_intent: Build a daily quest system with 3 time-window sub-quests (morning 6-12, afternoon 12-18, evening 18-24) — each available ONLY in its window, with bonus reward for completing all 3. This forces multiple sessions per day (idle game retention magic). Egg Inc., Hay Day use this. Completing all 3 unlocks streak bonus.
mathematical_foundation: 3 time windows per day with non-overlap. Completing all 3 in single day = streak day +1. Streak rewards: linear days 1-7, exponential 7-30. Window enforcement server-side: quest available only if current_hour ∈ window AND no claim for this window today.
psychology_notes: Idle games depend on session count, not session length. 3 touchpoints/day = 3x dopamine moments. Window enforcement creates urgency ('I need to log in by 6pm or miss afternoon quest'). 'All 3 streak' adds gamification on top of gameplay.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player opens app. Server checks current time + window. Returns available quest if window active + not yet claimed. Player completes → next window's quest reveals at window start.

2. **Math**: Windows: 6:00-12:00, 12:00-18:00, 18:00-24:00 (no late-night to respect sleep). Player timezone respected.

3. **Edge cases**: Player only ever plays evenings — only gets 1/3 daily quests. Window straddle (player starts at 17:55, finishes at 18:05) — credited to start window.

4. **Visual**: 3-slot day visualizer (morning/afternoon/evening icons). Active window highlighted. 'Streak: 3-perfect days' indicator.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Server time only. Windows enforced server-side.

6. **Rejected**: Single daily (1 session), 24h rolling (no urgency), 5+ windows (notification fatigue).

database_design.schema: player_window_quests: player_id, date, morning_claimed, afternoon_claimed, evening_claimed, perfect_streak INT.

visualization_strategy: 3 slots with time-of-day icons. Active window pulses cyan. Claimed green. Inactive dimmed. Perfect-streak banner gold. Reduced motion: no pulse. Color-blind: state indicated by icon (✓) + label.
balance_considerations: Watch session count per day (target +50% vs single-window). Watch perfect-day rate (target 30-50% of dailies are perfect). If too low, window times misaligned with player patterns.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Timezone manipulation — server tz, 7-day cooldown on changes.
Window time edge cases — server boundary check authoritative.

game_genre_context: Trading card game with booster pack opening (Magic Arena, Hearthstone). Pack opening is THE ritual moment. Smart-pack (no-duplicates-until-full-set) was Magic's revolutionary 2019 addition.
title: Smart Pack with No-Duplicates-Until-Set-Complete and Wildcard Conversion
design_intent: Build a booster pack system that NEVER drops a card the player already has 4 of (max owned per card) until set is complete — auto-converts to wildcard. Magic Arena's wildcard system is the gold standard, killing pull frustration. Without it, players open 100 packs and get 60 duplicates.
mathematical_foundation: Pack of 8 cards: 1 rare/mythic (rare 7/8, mythic 1/8), 3 uncommon, 4 common. Duplicate-prevention: if rolled card already 4-owned, replace with same-rarity wildcard. Wildcard exchange rate: 1 wildcard = craft any card of same rarity. Crafting rare = 100 wildcard fragments OR 1 rare wildcard.
psychology_notes: Duplicate prevention removes 'this pack is wasted' rage. Wildcards as fallback give deterministic path to specific cards. Smart-pack converts F2P frustration into engagement.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player opens pack. Server rolls cards. For each, check player's count — if ≥4, swap for wildcard. Insert into inventory.

2. **Math**: 4-copy max is Magic standard for deck-building. Wildcard conversion 1:1 within rarity preserves pack value.

3. **Edge cases**: Player owns 100% of set already — every card is wildcard. Brand new player — no swaps until late in set acquisition.

4. **Visual**: Pack opening shows card → if duplicate-converted, smooth morph to wildcard with explanation.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All server-side.

6. **Rejected**: Raw RNG (Hearthstone pre-2024 — 80% duplicates by end of set), no wildcards (no F2P path).

balance_considerations: Watch duplicate-rate by player set-progress (rises smoothly to 100% as set completes). Watch crafting from wildcards (should be primary completion mechanism late in set).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Pack contents server-determined.
Wildcard inflation via bug — count check + audit log.

game_genre_context: Hardcore game (Dark Souls, Elden Ring) with rare achievement reveals shared on social. Steam global rarity creates community ('only 1% have this').
title: Global Rarity Achievement with Community Tracker and 'You Are The Xth' Reveal
design_intent: Build achievement system that tracks global unlock count in real-time, shows player 'You are the 4,237th player to do this' on unlock — Steam's global achievement rarity but more dramatic. Dark Souls's 'You Defeated' moment paired with rarity reveal creates shareable identity. Players screenshot their position.
mathematical_foundation: Global unlock count incremented atomically per achievement. Player position = count at time of unlock. Rarity percentage = unlock_count / total_players. Total_players approximated to avoid expensive count(*) every query.
psychology_notes: 'You are the Nth' is identity. Specific number > vague 'top 5%'. Early adopters of difficult achievements get bragging rights ('I was #47'). Late adopters get 'I'm part of the elite N% who made it'.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Achievement triggered. Atomic increment of global count. Player record gets count-at-time-of-unlock. Display shown to player.

2. **Math**: Atomic increment via Redis INCR. Player position fixed at moment, never updates.

3. **Edge cases**: Hot achievements (10k unlocks/min on launch) — Redis handles. Race conditions on position assignment — INCR is atomic.

4. **Visual**: Dramatic unlock screen with 'YOU ARE THE 4,237th' big number. Achievement name above. Share button.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All server-side.

6. **Rejected**: Per-player count (no community), end-of-day update (kills moment).

visualization_strategy: Sequential reveal: achievement name, then position, then rarity. Big numbers with gradient text. Sentence wraps around number ('You are the [4,237th]'). Reduced motion: instant all-at-once. Color-blind: rarity tier via text label, not just color.
balance_considerations: Watch share rate per unlock (target 5%+ for sub-1% rarity). Watch first-100-positions claim time (game launch competitive bragging).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Spoofing position — server INCR atomic.
Re-triggering for position update — ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING preserves original.

game_genre_context: Asynchronous casual game (Wordle, Heardle, daily puzzle apps). One puzzle per day for everyone. Leaderboard = how fast you solved today's puzzle. Massive social play despite no real-time interaction.
title: Daily Puzzle Leaderboard with Friend-Group Filter and Spoiler-Free Time Display
design_intent: Build a daily-puzzle leaderboard where everyone races the same puzzle, with friend-group filtering ('your group's times'), and SPOILER-FREE design — solve time visible, but never the answer. Wordle's social model. Showing scores without revealing solution = the genius.
mathematical_foundation: Per-day puzzle: same for all players globally. Leaderboard scored by solve_time_ms. Friend group: subset of global. Streak: consecutive daily solves. Spoiler protection: never expose puzzle answer in leaderboard data.
psychology_notes: Shared experience without spoilers = water-cooler-friendly. 'How'd you do today?' becomes a global ritual. Friend leaderboards drive group accountability.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Daily puzzle at midnight UTC (or rolling local). Player solves, submits time. Leaderboards updated. Friend filter applied on read.

2. **Math**: Single global puzzle = single leaderboard. Sort by time ASC.

3. **Edge cases**: Player solves multiple times — only first counts. Player on different timezone — daily roll respects.

4. **Visual**: Time displayed, NO answer shown. Friend group toggle prominent.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Solve validated via replay (clicks/inputs reproduce solution).

6. **Rejected**: Showing solution post-leaderboard (spoiler), allowing retries (gaming the time).

game_genre_context: Live-service game with limited-time events (Fortnite, Apex). Event start/end notifications drive concurrent peaks. One global broadcast event = simultaneous mass-notification challenge.
title: Mass Event Notification Broadcast with Stagger and Smart Geo-Routing
design_intent: Build a mass-notification system for 'Event starting in 10 min!' that staggers send to 10M players over 60 seconds (to spread server load + avoid Apple/Google rate limits), geo-routes to players' awake hours, and respects opt-outs. Fortnite's event broadcasts must hit globally without melting infrastructure.
mathematical_foundation: Stagger window: 60s. Players bucketed into 60 1-second waves randomly. Apple APNS limit: 9000 notifications/sec/team. Google FCM: similar. Total throughput needed for 10M in 60s: 167k/sec — exceed limits without staggering. Geo-routing: filter to players in 'awake' window (8am-10pm local).
psychology_notes: Event broadcasts drive concurrent peaks — Fortnite events have 12M concurrent. Mass notification 5 minutes before event = surge. Mass at 3am local = no impact + uninstalls.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Event manager triggers broadcast. Worker bucketizes recipient list, schedules waves 1s apart. Each wave fires to its bucket. Geo-filter applied per player.

2. **Math**: 10M / 60 = 167k per wave; if FCM has 10 concurrent connections, 17k/sec/conn — within limits.

3. **Edge cases**: Player just installed mid-broadcast — gets next wave or in-app notice. Notification fails mid-broadcast — retry queue.

4. **Visual**: In-app banner takes over from FOMO push.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Players can't trigger broadcasts (admin only).

6. **Rejected**: Simultaneous fire (rate limit hits), single 8am UTC broadcast (kills APAC + EU).

balance_considerations: Watch concurrent-player peak at event start (target 2-3x normal). Watch broadcast send-success rate (target 99%+). Watch uninstalls within 24h of broadcast — should be flat (not spiked).
exploit_vectors_addressed: Mass spam (admin-only triggers) — RBAC enforced.
Notification flooding individual player — broadcast bypasses normal throttling but only fires once per event.

game_genre_context: Mobile/web hybrid game (Hearthstone, Marvel Snap) with seasonal cosmetic shop rotation. Players have FOMO budget — they save currency for items they want, then watch them rotate out.
title: Rotating Cosmetic Shop with Personal Wishlist and Smart Notification on Wishlist Item Live
design_intent: Build a shop with weekly cosmetic rotation, a player wishlist (items players favorited), and smart notification when a wishlisted item comes into stock. Magic Arena's wildcards shop does something similar; Fortnite's daily rotation creates the urgency, but no wishlist tracker — players miss items they wanted. Combining = revenue lift.
mathematical_foundation: Shop rotation: 8 items per day from pool of 200+. Items reappear ~ every 4 weeks (probabilistic). Wishlist items prioritized when they appear: client banner + push notification. Conversion lift from notification: 40%+ vs ambient browsing.
psychology_notes: Wishlist = commitment ('I want that'). Notification at availability = closure ('the moment I was waiting for'). Without wishlist, FOMO is generic; with wishlist, it's personal.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player browses items, hits 'wishlist'. Daily rotation picks items, system checks wishlists, sends notification to those with wishlisted item live.

2. **Math**: Rotation weighted to surface wishlisted items slightly more often (don't overdo it — feels manipulative).

3. **Edge cases**: Player wishlists 100 items — cap at 20 to maintain meaning. Item delisted from game — remove from wishlists.

4. **Visual**: Shop with star icon to wishlist. 'Wishlisted item live!' banner. Push notification with item image.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Wishlist tied to account.

6. **Rejected**: No wishlist (miss FOMO), notify-on-every-rotation (spam).

game_genre_context: Speedrunning leaderboard site (speedrun.com style). Stats for runs: split times, world record comparisons, pace tracking. Speedrun stats are deeply granular.
title: Speedrun Split Tracker with Pace Calculator and PB-vs-WR Diff Visualization
design_intent: Build a per-run split tracker where players record times per segment, compare to personal best AND world record, with live 'on pace' or 'behind pace' indicator during live runs. LiveSplit (open source) is the model. Splits + pace is the speedrun experience.
mathematical_foundation: Splits: array of (segment_name, segment_time_ms). Cumulative_time at segment N = sum(times[0..N]). Pace_diff at segment N = current_cum - pb_cum_at_N. 'Sum of best' = sum of fastest-ever per-segment times (theoretical optimum).
psychology_notes: Splits make every segment a mini-race. Pace diff during live runs creates moment-by-moment drama. Sum-of-best is aspirational goal.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player starts run, server marks start. On each split, send timestamp, server computes time. Compare to PB + WR. Display pace diff.

2. **Math**: All math client-side once server provides PB+WR baseline.

3. **Edge cases**: Player skips segment — invalid run. Server crashed mid-run — client buffer continues, sync on reconnect.

4. **Visual**: Split list with deltas color-coded. Live pace bar. PB+WR ghosts.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Submitted runs validated (server timestamp deltas).

6. **Rejected**: Total time only (no granularity).

game_genre_context: Asymmetric multiplayer (Dead by Daylight, Evolve, Identity V) where one player has different role/power than others. Matching 1 hunter vs 4 survivors must balance both sides.
title: Asymmetric Matchmaker with Per-Role Skill Ratings and Queue-Balance Incentives
design_intent: Build a matchmaker for 1v4 asymmetric games: 1 killer vs 4 survivors. Killers and survivors have separate ELO ratings. Queue often has 5x more survivors than killers (killers are 'harder'). System incentivizes killers via bonus rewards when queue is unbalanced. Dead by Daylight's exact model.
mathematical_foundation: Per-role rating tracked separately. Match formation: 1 killer + 4 survivors at similar rating. Imbalance bonus: when killer:survivor queue ratio > 1:5, killers earn 2x XP (incentivize role-switching). Queue time: scales with imbalance.
psychology_notes: Asymmetric gameplay creates 'I want to be the killer' vs 'I want to be the survivor' identity. Rating per role respects that someone might be killer-Gold and survivor-Silver. Imbalance bonus shifts queue dynamics organically.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player queues with role. Matchmaker scans both queues, forms 1+4 lobbies at matched rating. Imbalance ratio monitored; bonus toggled.

2. **Math**: ELO per role. Imbalance bonus thresholds tuned.

3. **Edge cases**: New player has no role rating — placement matches. Killer queue starvation — fallback to wider rating tolerance.

4. **Visual**: Queue UI shows estimated wait per role + bonus indicator.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Role-switch for bonus farming detected (frequent role swaps without play).

6. **Rejected**: Single rating across roles (broken), no bonus (queue collapse).

database_design.schema: player_role_rating: player_id, role TEXT, rating NUMERIC, games_played. queue_metrics_snapshots: snapshot_at, killer_queue_count, survivor_queue_count.

visualization_strategy: Role choice with descriptions. Bonus badge gold-on-yellow when active — incentive visible. Survivor queue note 'longer right now' transparent. Reduced motion: standard.
balance_considerations: Watch role queue balance (target 1:4 to 1:5). Watch bonus effectiveness — how many players switch when bonus active. Track per-role rating distribution.
exploit_vectors_addressed: Bonus farming via role-swap without playing — bonus only on completed matches.
Queue-dodging — penalty per role.

game_genre_context: Speed-chess online tournaments (Lichess Arena style) — fast-paced, many simultaneous players, automatic pairings continuously throughout window. Arena-style differs from single-elimination.
title: Continuous Arena Tournament with Live Pairing and Streak-Based Berserker Mode
design_intent: Build a Lichess-style Arena: players queue up, get paired immediately on availability, with streak bonus (2 wins in a row = 2x points). 1-hour window, continuous play. The arena IS the meta — speedrun your win count. Lichess's Arena format drives daily community engagement.
mathematical_foundation: Player paired with similar-rated available player. Win = 2 points, draw = 1, loss = 0. Streak: 2+ wins in a row = next win counts 2x (4 points). Top score at end wins.
psychology_notes: Continuous pairing = no downtime = hooked. Streak bonus = momentum reward. Public ranking updates live = adrenaline.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Tournament window opens. Players check in. Server pairs available players continuously. Games scored. Leaderboard live-updated.

2. **Math**: Streak counter resets on loss/draw. Pairing minimizes rating diff among ready players.

3. **Edge cases**: Player AFK between games — un-queued after 30s timeout. Late entry — joins live, no penalty.

4. **Visual**: Live leaderboard updating per game. Personal score + streak indicator. Auto-queue toggle.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Pairing server-controlled. Quick-quit detected.

6. **Rejected**: Pre-scheduled brackets (rigid), no streak bonus (no momentum).

game_genre_context: Long-running live-service game (Destiny 2, Warframe) where players miss seasons or return after years. Veteran-friendly catch-up is critical for returning player retention.
title: Returning Player Catch-Up with Compressed Season Replay and Cosmetic Time Capsule
design_intent: Build a returning-player onboarding that compresses missed seasons into '90 days of content in 1 week', with cosmetic time capsule offering to PURCHASE missed cosmetics (with discount). Destiny 2 and Warframe deliberately support returning players because they're the highest LTV cohort. Don't make them feel left behind.
mathematical_foundation: Missed seasons identified (last_active_season < current_season - 1). Catch-up compression: 4x XP gain for 14 days. Time capsule pricing: missed cosmetics 50% off original (apologizing for absence). Quest chain: condensed story recap → present.
psychology_notes: Returning players have nostalgia + worry. 'Did I miss everything?' is the question. Showing 'here's everything that happened + you can still get it' answers anxiety.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Returning player detected (>30 days inactive). System auto-grants 4x XP buff, opens time-capsule shop with missed cosmetics.

2. **Math**: 4x XP for 14 days = compressed catch-up. 50% off pricing tested via A/B.

3. **Edge cases**: Player came back, then disappeared again — buff persists until consumed. Cosmetic from current season — full price, not discounted.

4. **Visual**: 'Welcome back' modal with timeline of missed seasons. Time-capsule shop with golden 'limited 50% off' badges.

5. **Anti-exploit**: 'Returning' status server-determined.

6. **Rejected**: No catch-up (returning players quit again), free missed cosmetics (devalues regular play).

game_genre_context: Co-op puzzle game (Portal 2, We Were Here, It Takes Two) requiring tight 2-player synchronization. Players must see each other's actions in real-time even if separated by 'world'.
title: Two-Player Coop Sync with Independent Views and Shared State Channel
design_intent: Build a 2-player co-op state-sync where each player can be in a DIFFERENT room/area, sees own world independently, but shared puzzle state syncs across (pulling lever in room A opens door in room B). Portal 2 co-op is the model. Latency tolerance must be very tight for puzzle synchronization.
mathematical_foundation: Local state (player position, look angle) updated 60Hz client-side. Shared state (puzzle solutions, room states) authoritative on server, broadcast on change. Latency budget: <100ms for shared state propagation. Conflict resolution: last-write-wins for non-puzzle elements; puzzle state uses semaphore-locked update.
psychology_notes: Co-op puzzle = communication. Latency = miscommunication. Tight sync = trust. Trust = retention.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: 2 players connect. Both load same world. Independent view per player. Server tracks shared state. Action on shared object → server validates → broadcasts to both.

2. **Math**: Local prediction acceptable for player movement; shared state requires server confirmation.

3. **Edge cases**: One player disconnects mid-puzzle — state preserved 5 min for reconnect. Both players act on same lever simultaneously — first action wins, second sees update.

4. **Visual**: 'Partner is here' indicator. Shared object highlights when partner interacts.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Shared state server-authoritative.

6. **Rejected**: Full state sync (bandwidth), client-authoritative (race conditions).

game_genre_context: Mob/enemy generator for action game (Diablo, Path of Exile) where each region has unique mobs with affixes (Frozen, Burning, Vampiric). Procedural mobs prevent same-encounter fatigue.
title: Enemy Affix Generator with Balance-Constrained Multi-Affix Combinations
design_intent: Build a system where enemies in higher difficulties get 1-4 random affixes that modify behavior (Frozen=slows player on hit, Burning=damage over time, Vampiric=heals on hit, Teleporting=moves randomly). Combinations must be balanced — Frozen+Teleporting+Vampiric creates uncatchable killing machine. Diablo III's elite mob system is the model.
mathematical_foundation: Affix pool of 20+. Per-enemy: roll 1-4 affixes (more for higher tier monsters). Forbidden combinations: pre-declared incompatibility matrix (Teleporting + Vampiric forbidden — heals while you can't catch). Power score: sum of affix power_values; cap at threshold.
psychology_notes: Procedural enemies = surprise. Every elite encounter is unique. Balance constraints prevent rage-quit unwinnable combos.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Mob spawned. Server rolls affix count + selection. Validates against forbidden matrix + power cap. Stamps to mob.

2. **Math**: Power scaling per affix.

3. **Edge cases**: All affixes pre-validated as fair. Boss encounters use curated affix sets.

4. **Visual**: Affix icons above enemy nameplate. Color glow per primary affix.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Affix server-determined.

6. **Rejected**: Random no-constraint (unwinnable combos), single affix (boring).

game_genre_context: Real-time strategy (StarCraft 2, AoE IV) where HUD shows minimap, resource counts, unit selection, queue. Information density is the genre's defining UI challenge.
title: RTS HUD with Minimap Click-Routing and Selection Group Hotkeys
design_intent: Build an RTS HUD with: zoomable minimap with click-to-route, unit selection group hotkeys (1-9 for custom groups), resource tickers, current production queue. StarCraft 2's HUD is information-density mastery. Every screen pixel earns its keep.
mathematical_foundation: Minimap viewport = main_camera_bounds rendered into minimap rect. Click on minimap → camera pan to that location. Selection group: 1 hotkey, 0-9 — stores unit IDs. Reselect on press, append on shift+press.
psychology_notes: RTS skill = micromanagement. HUD that minimizes mouse-travel = competitive advantage. Pros memorize HUD geometry — predictable layout > pretty layout.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Minimap renders compressed world. Click maps to world coord. Selection group hotkeys store/recall unit lists.

2. **Math**: Minimap scaling factor = map_size / minimap_size. Click pos in minimap × inverse = world pos.

3. **Edge cases**: Selected units die — auto-remove from group. Multiple production queues — show all.

4. **Visual**: Compressed but readable minimap (units as 1-2 pixel dots, color by alignment). Production queue thumbnails. Resource icons + counts top-left.

5. **Anti-exploit**: HUD purely cosmetic.

6. **Rejected**: Hidden minimap (kills awareness), large minimap (blocks gameplay).

game_genre_context: Web-based 3D customization (Avatar Maker, character creator). 3D model with swappable parts, materials, accessories. Player attachment to avatar drives long-term engagement.
title: Real-Time Character Customizer with Modular Mesh Swapping and Material Editing
design_intent: Build a character customizer where players swap hair, clothes, accessories on a 3D model, with real-time material/color editing. Combinatorial space is huge (1000s of looks). Cyberpunk 2077 character creator and Final Fantasy XIV's are the standard. Web implementation must be performant.
mathematical_foundation: Modular meshes attached to skeleton bones. Material color via shader uniform updates (no texture re-bake). Hue rotation via HSL color space transform. Memory: ~50MB for all options loaded lazily.
psychology_notes: Avatar = identity. Customization = self-expression. Players who customize deeply have 3x retention vs default-avatar players.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Load base character. Player picks part (hair, top, etc.). Replace mesh. Adjust color via slider — shader uniform update.

2. **Math**: Color space transforms for hue. Bone-attachment for mesh swap.

3. **Edge cases**: Mesh swap during animation — re-bind skin. Color value out of valid range — clamp.

4. **Visual**: 3D preview rotatable. Part categories tabbed. Color picker with palette presets.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Customization purely cosmetic.

6. **Rejected**: Pre-rendered head shots only (no real customization), unique mesh per look (storage explosion).

game_genre_context: PvP guild war game (Lineage M, Lords Mobile) with declared guild rivalries that span seasons. Long-form guild stories create meta-narratives — 'Red Skulls vs Blue Wolves' becomes lore.
title: Guild Rivalry System with Persistent History and Bragging-Rights Stat Tracking
design_intent: Build a rivalry system where two guilds can declare official rivals (mutual opt-in), tracking their head-to-head history across wars, MVPs, comebacks. Long-form data becomes lore. Lords Mobile and Black Desert guilds have informal rivalries; making it official + tracked turns it into content.
mathematical_foundation: Rivalry: pair (guild_a, guild_b) with start_date. Tracked: total wars, win/loss split, biggest victory margin, longest streak. Stats persist across seasons. Public 'rivalry score' = win_streak * 10 + dominance_score.
psychology_notes: Named enemies create identity. Tracked history creates lore. Big rivalry wins become 'I was there' stories — community gold.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Guild leader proposes rivalry. Other accepts. Stats start tracking. Every war between them counts double for rivalry stats.

2. **Math**: Win streak resets on loss. Dominance based on score differential.

3. **Edge cases**: Guild disbands — rivalry suspended, can be revived if guild reforms. Multiple rivalries per guild — capped at 3.

4. **Visual**: Rivalry banner with both guild emblems, current head-to-head record. War-history timeline with notable matches.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Rivalry requires mutual consent.

6. **Rejected**: System-assigned rivals (no player agency), no history tracking (loses lore).

game_genre_context: Browser MMO (Town Star, Drakensang Online) where market manipulation via macros is the primary cheat vector — not damage hacks. Detecting non-human market trading patterns is the real challenge.
title: Market Bot Detection via Trading-Pattern Behavioral Analysis
design_intent: Build a detection system that identifies market bots by analyzing trading patterns: consistent millisecond-precision listing timing, exact same item rotations, never-touched UI elements. Bots starve players from market opportunities. EVE Online has dedicated bot-detection teams.
mathematical_foundation: Features: listing time-of-day variance (humans irregular, bots clockwork), inter-action delay distribution (humans 1-30s with variance, bots 5.001s ± 0.01s), item category diversity (humans varied, bots specialized), idle gaps (humans take breaks, bots don't). Bot score = weighted sum.
psychology_notes: Honest traders fight bots for opportunities and lose every time. Markets dominated by bots feel rigged. Removing bots = restored faith in economy.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: All market actions logged with timestamp. Background job analyzes patterns per player. High bot-score flagged for review.

2. **Math**: Std deviation of inter-action delays — humans 5+, bots <1. KS test against human baseline.

3. **Edge cases**: Power-trader human looks bot-like — manual review distinguishes. Bot operator does manual occasional clicks to mimic — multi-feature detection catches patterns.

4. **Visual**: No player UI. Bots silently restricted from market.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Detection thresholds tuned monthly.

6. **Rejected**: CAPTCHA on every market action (kills UX), ban-on-suspicion (false positives).

game_genre_context: Free-to-play mobile game with reward chest opening (Clash Royale chest unlock, Marvel Strike Force). Chests with timer-based unlocks create check-in loops.
title: Timer-Based Chest Unlock with Pay-to-Skip and Slot Management
design_intent: Build a chest unlock system: 4 chest slots, each unlocking on timer (1h common, 8h rare, 24h epic). Players queue chests, optionally pay gems to skip timer. Manage slot strategically. Clash Royale's chest system drives session loops AND monetization.
mathematical_foundation: Slot count: 4. Tier-based unlock times: common 1h, rare 4h, epic 8h, legendary 24h. Gem-to-skip cost: floor(remaining_minutes / 10) gems. Chests earned from gameplay queue into slots when slot available.
psychology_notes: Timer creates anticipation. Slot scarcity forces choices. Pay-to-skip converts impatience to revenue.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player wins match → chest reward. Add to first empty slot. If no slot, chest queues. Timer starts on slot fill (queue chests don't tick). Pay-to-skip optional.

2. **Math**: Skip cost is non-linear — cheap when timer almost done, expensive when fresh.

3. **Edge cases**: All slots full, queue full — chest lost (warn player) or downgrade currency.

4. **Visual**: 4 slot UI with timer bars + skip prices. Queue showing waiting chests.

5. **Anti-exploit**: All timers server-side.

6. **Rejected**: Infinite slots (no scarcity), no pay-skip (revenue loss).

game_genre_context: Hardcore game (Dark Souls, Elden Ring) where daily quest design clashes with the genre's ethos. Dark Souls FROM doesn't have daily quests — but indie souls-likes test 'optional dailies' for retention.
title: Optional Daily Quest with Hardcore-Friendly Framing and Lore-Integrated Objectives
design_intent: Build daily quests that DON'T break hardcore game immersion: optional, lore-framed ('A scholar requests proof of a great victory'), reward LORE not just stats, completion does NOT feel like a chore. Bloodborne's Chalice Dungeons did something similar — optional repeatable content for veterans.
mathematical_foundation: Daily quest pool of ~15. Each rewards small XP + lore unlock + niche cosmetic. Compounds to interesting reward over weeks. No daily streaks (hardcore audience hates pressure).
psychology_notes: Hardcore players resist mandatory dailies but accept OPTIONAL ones with story. Lore unlocks are valued more than power.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player visits in-game NPC. NPC offers quest. Player accepts (or not). Quest objective set. Completion grants lore + token reward.

2. **Math**: Pool rotation ensures variety. No exponential rewards (anti-grind).

3. **Edge cases**: Player ignores dailies for 100 days — fine, system is invisible. Player completes all 15 — pool replenishes (new quests from author).

4. **Visual**: NPC has subtle quest indicator (not screaming '!' marker). Lore reward shown in journal, not popup.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Standard quest validation.

6. **Rejected**: Mandatory dailies (genre mismatch), streak punishment (hardcore audience walks).

game_genre_context: Mobile gacha with seasonal premium currency events (Genshin Impact, Honkai Star Rail). Season passes intersect with gacha — completing pass earns gacha currency.
title: Battle Pass with Gacha-Currency Track and Whale-Friendly Express Pass
design_intent: Build a battle pass where free track unlocks gacha currency milestones, paid track ('Express Pass') unlocks the same milestones FASTER + extra exclusive gacha tickets. Genshin Impact's BP is the model. Critical: free path must still feel rewarding (Genshin's free track gives mostly cosmetics + small currency; premium adds 4 fates + the chest weapon = substantial).
mathematical_foundation: 50 tiers per season. Free track: ~1280 gacha currency + cosmetics. Premium track (express): same + 4 limited fates + featured 5-star weapon. Premium price: $10. Math: 4 fates = $24 value retail, weapon = $50+ value. Premium ROI: 7x for whales.
psychology_notes: Whale-targeted premium with overwhelming value drives conversion. Free track keeps F2P engaged without making them feel exploited.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Standard BP. Each tier rewards include gacha-relevant currency. Premium adds gacha-specific items (fates, weapons).

2. **Math**: Tuned so free track = ~10 gacha pulls equivalent, premium = ~40 pulls equivalent.

3. **Edge cases**: Premium purchased late — retroactive grants. Currency hits gacha cap — overflow converts to lesser currency.

4. **Visual**: Track shows fates/currency milestones with emphasis on premium-only items.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Standard BP anti-exploit.

6. **Rejected**: Free track no gacha currency (F2P quits), premium no exclusive items (no buy reason).

visualization_strategy: Free vs premium track visually distinct. Gacha tickets highlighted (whale-bait). Locked premium dimmed but visible (FOMO).
balance_considerations: Watch premium conversion (target 30%+ in gacha games). F2P pull-equivalent (target 10 pulls). Featured weapon claim rate.

game_genre_context: Brawler / arena game (Brawlhalla, Rivals of Aether) with 1v1 ranked AND team queues. Matchmaker must handle both pools without cannibalizing.
title: Multi-Queue Matchmaker with Cross-Pool Solo Backfill
design_intent: Build a matchmaker supporting 1v1 ranked, 2v2 ranked, and casual modes. Solo players can backfill 2v2 missing partners. Pool segregation prevents 1v1 grinders skewing 2v2 rating. Brawlhalla nails multi-queue; Rivals of Aether 2 launch had queue-split issues that fragmented the playerbase.
mathematical_foundation: Per-mode rating. Cross-mode visibility: solos waiting for 1v1 can opt-in to backfill 2v2 if no 1v1 found in 30s. Backfill rating mapped via formula: rating_2v2 = rating_1v1 * 0.95 (slightly conservative).
psychology_notes: Multiple queues fragment small playerbases. Cross-pool backfill keeps queue times short for casual modes.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Player queues mode. Server scans matching pool. If no match in T seconds, optional backfill into alternate mode.

2. **Math**: Cross-rating mapping conservative (don't overrate from cross-pool).

3. **Edge cases**: Backfill player wins 2v2 — rating tracked separately, doesn't affect 1v1.

4. **Visual**: Queue UI shows estimated wait + backfill option.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Mode-specific rating prevents farming.

6. **Rejected**: Single queue (mode preference lost), no cross-fill (small playerbases starve).

Backfill abuse — opt-in only, rating tracked separately.

game_genre_context: In-game weekly tournament with prize-pool distribution (Brawlhalla Power Rankings, Brawl Stars Power League). Many concurrent tournaments, automated everything.
title: Automated Round-Robin League with Power Ranking Aggregation Across Weeks
design_intent: Build an automated round-robin league: 8 players per group play each other once per week, points awarded by placement, season aggregate across 8 weeks determines top 16 for playoff. Aggregate prevents single-bad-week elimination. Brawlhalla Power Rankings and online chess leagues use this.
mathematical_foundation: Group of 8 = 7 matches per week per player. Points: 1st=10, 2nd=7, 3rd=5, 4th=3, 5-8th=1. Season aggregate sum. Tiebreaker: head-to-head, then point differential.
psychology_notes: Weekly cadence creates appointment. Aggregate forgives bad days. Playoff cut drives final-weeks drama.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: League season starts. Players assigned groups. Each week, round-robin auto-scheduled. Points tallied. End of season cuts top 16.

2. **Math**: Round-robin combinatorics: 8 players = 28 matches per group per season (4 weeks of 7 matches each).

3. **Edge cases**: Player drops mid-season — remaining matches forfeit. Late join — joins next week.

4. **Visual**: Standings table per group. Match history. Cut-line indicator.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Match validation server-side.

6. **Rejected**: Single elimination per week (kills aggregate concept), pure aggregate (single bad week eliminates).

visualization_strategy: Above-cut rows green-tinted. Cut line clearly marked. Standings sortable. Reduced motion: no animations.
balance_considerations: Watch participation rate per group. Watch dropout rate post-bad-week (target <10%).

Forfeit gaming — penalty applied.

game_genre_context: Open-world game with procedurally generated quests / contracts (The Witcher 3 contracts, Cyberpunk 2077 gigs). Procedural quest text + objectives appear hand-crafted.
title: Procedural Contract Quest Generator with Template-Based Narrative and Validated Objectives
design_intent: Build a quest generator producing 'find X in Y for Z reward' quests with procedural narrative text (template + Markov-chain prose), validated objectives (target NPCs exist, locations reachable), and reward scaling. Witcher 3 contracts feel hand-written; many are templated. Bethesda's Radiant quest system is the inspiration but bigger.
mathematical_foundation: Templates: 'Kill X (count) of Y (creature) at Z (location) for W (reward)'. Variables filled from pools. Narrative wrapper generated via Markov chain trained on hand-authored examples. Reward scales: base * difficulty_multiplier.
psychology_notes: Procedural quests give infinite content. Good ones feel handcrafted; bad ones feel like AI slop. Validation is the difference.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Quest template chosen. Variables filled from validated pools (NPC exists at location, creature spawns in zone). Narrative text generated. Quest activated.

2. **Math**: Template-pool combinatorics give thousands of variations. Markov narrative adds prose variety.

3. **Edge cases**: Target NPC died — quest invalidated. Location now inaccessible — quest re-routed.

4. **Visual**: Quest journal with generated text indistinguishable from hand-authored.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Quests don't grant exploits.

6. **Rejected**: Pure random (gibberish), only hand-authored (limited content).

game_genre_context: Card game with deck-tracking (Hearthstone, Magic Arena). Players want to know which decks win against which, build percentages, and meta breakdowns.
title: Deck-Tracker Meta Analytics with Matchup Matrix and Counter Suggestion
design_intent: Build a deck meta analytics dashboard showing top 20 decks, their win rates, matchup matrix (which beats which), and counter-suggestion ('your deck struggles vs Zoo Warlock — try cards X, Y'). HSReplay and HSGuru built businesses on this for Hearthstone. Untapped.gg for Magic.
mathematical_foundation: Deck archetypes detected via clustering (K-means on card-list vectors). Matchup win rate: matches_won / total_matches per (deck_a, deck_b) pair. Counter suggestion: cards with highest win-rate-when-included against target archetype.
psychology_notes: Meta knowledge is competitive edge. Tools that surface meta = community engagement. Tools that suggest counters = strategic empowerment.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Match data ingested. Decks clustered to archetypes. Matchup stats aggregated. Counter analysis runs nightly.

2. **Math**: K-means clustering on card-inclusion vectors. Matchup stats need min sample (50+ matches).

3. **Edge cases**: New archetype emerges — clustering re-runs, may take days to stabilize.

4. **Visual**: Meta tier list. Matchup heatmap (green=favorable, red=unfavorable). Card suggestions ranked.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Data from server-validated matches.

6. **Rejected**: Manual archetype tagging (doesn't scale), no matchup data (less actionable).

visualization_strategy: Tier list ranked. Matchup matrix uses green-to-red gradient for favorable-to-unfavorable. Heatmap immediately readable. Reduced motion. Color-blind: matchup % shown as numbers + color.
balance_considerations: Watch meta diversity (Gini coefficient of play-rate distribution). Watch tool usage as proxy for engagement.

game_genre_context: Web-based fishing game (Webfishing) or driving game (slither.io-style 3D). Simple 3D physics in browser. Performance-conscious physics tuning.
title: Lightweight 3D Physics Loop for Browser Games with Spatial Hashing
design_intent: Build a physics loop handling 500+ moving entities at 60fps in browser using spatial hashing for collisions. Cannon.js / Rapier are options but heavyweight; custom solutions for simple games are faster.
mathematical_foundation: Spatial hash: divide world into cells, hash entity positions. Collision check only entities in same cell + neighbors. O(N) vs O(N²). Integration: simple Euler with substeps for stability.
psychology_notes: 60fps physics = smooth gameplay = retention.
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Each frame: update entity positions, rebuild spatial hash, check collisions within cells, resolve.

2. **Math**: Cell size = 2x typical entity size. Substep count tuned per collision frequency.

3. **Edge cases**: Entity at cell boundary — check neighbors. High-speed entity — substep to prevent tunneling.

4. **Visual**: Smooth motion, no jitter.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Physics server-side for competitive.

6. **Rejected**: Cannon.js (heavyweight), no broad-phase (O(N²) crashes).

visualization_strategy: Entities render at physics positions. Smooth 60fps.
balance_considerations: Watch FPS by entity count. Cell size tuning per game.

game_genre_context: Cross-platform social game (Roblox-like UGC platforms) with friend systems spanning desktop + mobile. Friend status sync across devices is the engineering challenge.
title: Cross-Device Friend Status Sync with Presence Indicator and Activity Feed
design_intent: Build a friend presence system: 'online (mobile)', 'in match (PC)', 'offline 3h'. Activity feed shows friend events ('Alex unlocked Achievement X 5m ago'). Roblox and Discord's presence systems are the model — real-time status that respects privacy preferences.
mathematical_foundation: Presence stored as { online: bool, device, status_text, last_active }. Updated on session start/end and heartbeat. Activity feed: append-only log, viewable by friends only. Privacy: opt-out per friend or globally.
psychology_notes: Knowing friends are online creates 'I should play with them' impulse. Activity feed creates social proof ('Alex played the new dungeon — I should too').
reasoning:
1. **Flow**: Session start → presence updated. Heartbeat every 30s. Activity events appended. Friends see updates via WebSocket.

2. **Math**: Online definition: last_active < 60s.

3. **Edge cases**: User on multiple devices — last-active wins for online status, but device shown is most recent.

4. **Visual**: Friend list with status dots. Activity feed scrollable.

5. **Anti-exploit**: Privacy opt-out respected.

6. **Rejected**: Polling (high load), no activity feed (loses social glue).

database_design.schema: player_presence: player_id, online BOOLEAN, device TEXT, status_text TEXT, last_active TIMESTAMPTZ. activity_feed: id, player_id, event_type, event_data JSONB, created_at, visible_to_friends BOOLEAN.
database_design.indexes: (player_id) on presence. (player_id, created_at DESC) on feed.

visualization_strategy: Online status indicator on avatar — universal pattern. Device + status text below name. Activity feed concise. Reduced motion: standard. Color-blind: online status via color + dot border.
balance_considerations: Watch friend-trigger conversion rate (friend online → match started together). Watch privacy opt-out rate.

Activity scraping — friends-only by default.
